title: Understanding Medicare: A Complete Guide
summary: Learn about Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D, enrollment periods, and how to choose the right plan for your needs.

content.heading: What is Medicare?
content.text: Medicare is a federal health insurance program for people 65 and older, and certain younger people with disabilities. It covers hospital stays, doctor visits, prescription drugs, and more.

content.heading: Medicare Parts Explained
content.text: Part A covers hospital insurance. Part B covers medical insurance. Part C (Medicare Advantage) bundles A and B with extra benefits. Part D covers prescription drugs.

content.heading: When to Enroll
content.text: Your Initial Enrollment Period starts 3 months before your 65th birthday and ends 3 months after. Missing this window may result in late enrollment penalties.

content.heading: Getting Help
content.text: Free Medicare counseling is available through your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP). Lumis can connect you with a certified counselor.

title: Fall Prevention Tips for Seniors
summary: Falls are the leading cause of injury among seniors. Learn practical steps to reduce your risk and keep your home safe.

content.heading: Home Safety Checklist
content.text: Remove tripping hazards like loose rugs and clutter. Install grab bars in the bathroom. Ensure good lighting in hallways and staircases. Use non-slip mats in the shower.

content.heading: Stay Active
content.text: Regular exercise improves balance and strength. Consider tai chi, gentle yoga, or walking programs designed for seniors.

content.heading: Review Your Medications
content.text: Some medications can cause dizziness or drowsiness. Ask your doctor or pharmacist to review your prescriptions for fall-risk side effects.

content.heading: Vision and Footwear
content.text: Get regular eye exams and update your glasses. Wear well-fitting, non-slip shoes both indoors and outdoors.

title: Healthy Eating for Seniors
summary: Good nutrition is essential for healthy aging. Discover tips for maintaining a balanced diet and managing common dietary challenges.

content.heading: Key Nutrients for Seniors
content.text: Focus on calcium and vitamin D for bone health, B12 for energy, fiber for digestion, and omega-3 fatty acids for heart and brain health.

content.heading: Staying Hydrated
content.text: Seniors are more prone to dehydration. Aim for 6-8 glasses of water daily. Set reminders if you tend to forget.

content.heading: Managing Dietary Restrictions
content.text: If you have diabetes, heart disease, or other conditions, work with a dietitian to create a meal plan that meets your nutritional needs while managing your health.

content.heading: Meal Assistance Programs
content.text: If cooking is difficult, consider home-delivered meal programs or community dining options. Lumis can help you find services in your area.

title: Protecting Yourself from Scams
summary: Seniors are often targeted by scammers. Learn how to recognize and avoid common scams targeting older adults.

content.heading: Common Scams Targeting Seniors
content.text: Watch out for phone scams (fake IRS calls, tech support), email phishing, romance scams, Medicare fraud, and grandparent scams where callers pretend to be a grandchild in trouble.

content.heading: Warning Signs
content.text: Be suspicious of unsolicited calls asking for money or personal info, pressure to act immediately, requests for gift cards or wire transfers, and offers that seem too good to be true.

content.heading: How to Protect Yourself
content.text: Never give personal information to unknown callers. Don't click links in suspicious emails. Verify requests by calling the organization directly using a known number.

content.heading: Reporting Scams
content.text: Report suspected scams to the FTC at 1-877-FTC-HELP, your local police, and the Elder Fraud Hotline at 1-833-FRAUD-11.

title: Staying Socially Connected
summary: Social isolation can impact health. Discover ways to stay connected with family, friends, and your community.

content.heading: Why Connection Matters
content.text: Social isolation increases the risk of depression, cognitive decline, and heart disease. Regular social interaction is as important as physical exercise for healthy aging.

content.heading: Ways to Stay Connected
content.text: Join a senior center or community group. Volunteer for a cause you care about. Take a class or workshop. Attend community dining programs.

content.heading: Using Technology
content.text: Video calls, social media, and messaging apps can help you stay in touch with family. Many libraries and senior centers offer free technology classes.

content.heading: Companion Programs
content.text: If you're feeling isolated, companion visit programs can match you with a friendly visitor. Contact Lumis to learn about options in your area.

title: A Guide for Family Caregivers
summary: Caregiving can be rewarding but challenging. Find tips, resources, and support to help you care for your loved one while taking care of yourself.

content.heading: Understanding Your Role
content.text: As a caregiver, you may help with daily activities, medical care, finances, and emotional support. It's important to understand both your loved one's needs and your own limits.

content.heading: Avoiding Burnout
content.text: Take regular breaks using respite care. Join a support group. Ask for help from family and friends. Don't try to do everything alone.

content.heading: Legal and Financial Planning
content.text: Help your loved one prepare advance directives, power of attorney, and organize important documents. Consult a senior legal aid service for guidance.

content.heading: Resources for Caregivers
content.text: Lumis offers caregiver support programs, respite care referrals, and training workshops. Call our helpline to get connected.

title: Safe Exercise for Seniors
summary: Stay active and healthy with exercises designed for older adults. Learn about safe routines that improve strength, balance, and flexibility.

content.heading: Benefits of Exercise
content.text: Regular physical activity helps maintain mobility, reduces fall risk, improves mood, strengthens bones, and helps manage chronic conditions like diabetes and arthritis.

content.heading: Recommended Activities
content.text: Walking, swimming, tai chi, chair exercises, and gentle stretching are excellent low-impact options. Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate activity per week.

content.heading: Getting Started Safely
content.text: Consult your doctor before starting a new exercise routine. Start slowly, warm up before activity, and listen to your body. Stop if you feel pain or dizziness.

content.heading: Finding Exercise Programs
content.text: Many senior centers, YMCAs, and community centers offer free or low-cost exercise classes for older adults. Ask Lumis for programs near you.

title: Senior Housing Options Explained
summary: From aging in place to assisted living, understand the different housing options available for seniors and how to choose the right one.

content.heading: Aging in Place
content.text: Many seniors prefer to stay in their own homes. Home modifications, in-home care services, and community support can make this possible safely.

content.heading: Independent Living Communities
content.text: These offer private apartments with optional services like meals, housekeeping, and social activities. Ideal for active seniors who want a maintenance-free lifestyle.

content.heading: Assisted Living Facilities
content.text: Assisted living provides personal care assistance, meals, and activities in a residential setting. It's suitable for seniors who need daily help but not full-time nursing care.

content.heading: Paying for Senior Housing
content.text: Options include personal savings, long-term care insurance, Medicaid (for qualifying individuals), and VA benefits for veterans. A financial counselor can help you plan.
